abstract: Understanding the atomic-level structure of electrocatalysts under operating conditions is essential for identifying the mechanisms that drive their performance. In this study, we employ in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to investigate the structural evolution of Pt3Ni nanoframes, a class of highly efficient catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). By utilizing electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition and carbon monoxide electrooxidation, we demonstrate that nanoframes with distinct surface properties exhibit varying levels of binding strength to reaction intermediates. While the formation of a "Pt-skin" is known to optimize ORR activity by modulating surface binding energies, our results show that this process is highly dependent on initial platinum segregation. Specifically, nanoframes with lower activity possess rougher surfaces and higher Ni content near the surface, leading to deleterious Ni dissolution. In contrast, high-performance nanoframes exhibit a more pronounced segregation of platinum over a nickel-rich subsurface, facilitating the formation of a stable, high-quality Pt-skin. These findings highlight that the exceptional catalytic activity of Pt3Ni nanoframes originates from the successful atomic-level engineering of the Pt-skin structure, providing critical insights for the rational design of next-generation fuel cell catalysts.
